title: Pin, Common Pitfalls, and Best Practices
content:
Pin is Rust's way of saying 'this value will not move in memory.' Async futures often contain self-referential data (local variables that reference each other across await points), so they cannot be safely moved after being polled.

You rarely need to use Pin directly -- tokio::spawn and Box::pin handle it for you. But understanding Pin helps when you hit compiler errors about 'value does not implement Unpin.'

Common async pitfalls:
1. Holding a MutexGuard across .await -- this can deadlock or block the runtime thread.
2. Blocking the executor with CPU-heavy work or std::thread::sleep -- use tokio::task::spawn_blocking instead.
3. Creating futures that are not Send -- spawned tasks must be Send for multi-threaded runtimes.
4. Forgetting that futures are lazy -- calling an async fn without awaiting does nothing.
keyPoints: Pin prevents moving a value in memory -- needed because async futures can be self-referential
Most of the time Box::pin() or tokio::spawn handle pinning for you
Never hold a mutex lock across an .await point
Never call blocking functions (std::thread::sleep, heavy CPU work) in async context
Use tokio::task::spawn_blocking() for CPU-bound or blocking operations
Spawned tasks must be Send -- use Arc instead of Rc, and avoid non-Send types across awaits
Futures are lazy: always .await or spawn them, or they do nothing
